content:
{
  "title": "Adversarial-Contrastive Prompting: Improving LLM Robustness via Dynamic Contextual Contrast",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) are vulnerable to adversarial prompts and jailbreak attempts that elicit harmful or unauthorized outputs. Existing static prompting and filtering methods often fail against new or adaptive attacks.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in critical and public-facing applications, robust safeguards against adversarial misuse are essential. Recent jailbreak techniques can bypass standard prompt engineering and safety filters, necessitating novel, adaptive approaches to prompt design that proactively defend against such attacks.",
  "method": "We propose Adversarial-Contrastive Prompting (ACP), a dynamic prompting method that augments user queries with contrastive negative exemplars—synthetically generated adversarial prompts known to induce harmful behavior—alongside ideal positive exemplars. The LLM is instructed (via meta-prompting) to distinguish between benign and adversarial intent, conditioning its output on this contextual discrimination. At inference time, both the user prompt and a set of contrastive adversarial prompts are presented; the LLM must justify its refusal or compliance, referencing the context. This approach aims to activate internal safety mechanisms and improve robustness to novel attacks.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate ACP using the AdvBench and JailbreakBench datasets, comparing against vanilla prompting and self-consistency prompting. For each method, we will measure the attack success rate, refusal accuracy, and harmlessness using established metrics. The key comparison is whether ACP reduces attack success rates on previously unseen adversarial prompts relative to the strongest prior baseline."
}
